The film caused an uproar from conservative groups following its premiere at Studio 28 in Paris, resulting in... it being banned in France for 51 years.



The 4K restoration of L'Âge d'Or was carried out by La Cinémathèque française and the Centre Pompidou, MNAM-CCI/Service du cinéma expérimental, at the Hiventy laboratory for the image and at the L.E. Diapason studio for the sound, thanks to the support of Pathé and the Maison de Champagne Piper-Heidsieck, patrons of the Cinémathèque française. A duplicate set of files is kept at the Centre Pompidou. There is no film version of this restoration.



Preceded by: Un Chien Andalou

1929 | 16 min | France | Black-and-White | English intertitles | Not Rated | DCP



A seminal film in the cannon of Surrealist cinema, Luis Buñuel’s directorial debut is the first collaboration and brainchild between the filmmaker and Surrealist Salvador Dalí. While the film seemingly lacks a plot, it was inspired by a conversation Buñuel and Dalí had about dreams they had experienced. This exchange motivated them to write the irrational and often shocking sequences that make up the film. Using the medium to challenge traditional narrative structure, they explore the concept of repressed desire and the subconscious.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for two landmark Surrealist films that still possess their original power to disturb and provoke. The 4K restoration by La Cinémathèque française reveals new details in Buñuel's savage attack on bourgeois morality, while Un Chien Andalou remains cinema's most influential nightmare.

movies.title: Los Olvidados (The Young and the Damned)

ratings.plot: Made during Luis Buñuel’s prolific two-decade period in Mexico, Los olvidados won the country’s second major prize at Cannes, following the landmark win of María Candelaria (1944). This against-formula juvenile crime drama showcases the power of Indigenous actress Estela Inda, recipient of a Silver Ariel for Best Supporting Actress for her memorable performance as the mother of the film’s young, impressionable protagonist; both characters try their damnedest to rise above the crime festering in the slums of Mexico City.

verdict.reason: Essential Buñuel that strips away his surrealist flourishes to reveal a master filmmaker working in pure social realism. Estela Inda's Silver Ariel-winning performance as the desperate mother anchors one of cinema's most unflinching portraits of poverty.

movies.title: Simon of the Desert (with Land Without Bread)

ratings.plot:
Inspired by the fifth-century Syrian saint, renowned for his devotion to asceticism while living on the platform of a pillar, Simon of the Desert is Buñuel’s satirical take on the legend, executed with his signature surreal flair. When Simon (Claudio Brook) reaches a 6-year, 6-month, 6-day period of living in prayer at the top of a column, his faithfulness is challenged by the shape-shifting Satan (Silvia Pinal). Simon of the Desert was the last Mexican film production the director worked on, as well as his final collaboration with acclaimed cinematographer Gabriel Figueroa.



Preceded by:  Land Without Bread

1933 | 30 min | Spain | Black-and-White | French | Not Rated



Shooting for less than a month with borrowed equipment and editing the film himself on his kitchen table, Luis Buñuel made something close to a straightforward documentary in Land Without Bread, a lightly fictionalized document of the Las Hurdes region in the autonomous Spanish community of Extremadura. A stark portrait of extreme poverty and the population who refuses to leave a seemingly “breadless” land, the director originally provided live narration with its initial silent exhibitions, later adding sound through music and his own voice as one of the narrators.

verdict.reason: Essential Buñuel double bill that showcases his evolution from early documentary to late surrealist mastery. Figueroa's cinematography on the final 30-minute Simon makes for a perfect theatrical farewell to one of cinema's greatest collaborations.

ratings.plot: An unstable young woman escapes from a reformatory for seriously wayward girls and deceptively finds shelter in the kind home of a frighteningly nice and decent family. Little by little, she causes unrest and discord among the mem...

verdict.reason: Essential Buñuel at his most subversive, dismantling bourgeois morality with surgical precision. The director turns a simple morality tale into a razor-sharp satire that feels both timeless and bracingly modern.

ratings.plot: Among the strangest and most perturbing films of his overlooked Mexican period, ÉL is Luis Buñuel’s incisive portrait of paranoia, jealousy, and sexual obsession—a nightmarish tale of love gone wrong that prefigures the major themes of his 1960s and ’70s work. Incorporating his personal demons into an adaptation of Mercedes Pinto’s autobiographical novel, Buñuel tells the story of Francisco Galván de Montemayor (Arturo de Córdova), a devout middle-aged bachelor who falls into amour fou with Gloria (Delia Garcés). After breaking her engagement with another man, Gloria realizes something is terribly off about Francisco, whose sophisticated facade masks deep insecurities and an explosive, violent temper. Descending into madness, Francisco drives Gloria to fear for her life—with no refuge offered by either her family or the church. One of Buñuel’s rawest, angriest indictments of religious and social hypocrisy, ÉL stands as the surrealist master’s great excursion into dark melodrama, where civilization can find no answer to the raging urges of the irrational id.

verdict.reason: Buñuel's most psychologically brutal film, featuring Arturo de Córdova in a terrifying performance as a jealous husband. The church bell tower sequence alone justifies the theatrical experience.

movies.title: The Phantom of Liberty (Le Fantome de La Liberte)

ratings.plot: Following the success of The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie (1972), Luis Buñuel and Jean-Claude Carrière collaborated on another satirical comedy as co-writers, which became Buñuel’s penultimate cinematic work as director. Starring such greats as Julien Bertheau, Monica Vitti, and Jean-Claude Brialy, to name a few, this surrealist masterpiece comprises nonlinear episodes moving from the early 1800s during Napoleon’s colonial occupation in Spain and monks gambling while drinking and smoking to an upper-class gathering with guests seated on toilet seats. A scent of absurdity permeates the air of these episodes despite the naive earnestness of the characters.

verdict.reason: Late Buñuel at his most playfully subversive, worth seeing for the dinner party toilet scene alone. The film's connected vignettes flow with dream logic that rewards theatrical focus.

movies.title: Double Feature: Blues in The Night & Black Angel

ratings.plot:
Double Feature!


BLUES IN THE NIGHT

The first noir film to delve into the jazz world, Blues in the Night details the rise and inevitable fall of a St. Louis band. They land their big break when they get booked at a New Jersey nightclub, but the band’s leader, played by Richard Whorf, falls under the spell of the club’s femme fatale owner. It doesn’t take long for the other members to sour, setting off a chain reaction of jealousy, betrayal, and, eventually, murder. The supporting cast is superb, made up of familiar noir faces like Jack Carson, Howard Da Silva and Lloyd Nolan, while the screenplay by Robert Rossen makes good on the lyrics of the tragic, Oscar®-nominated title song. Future director Elia Kazan has his biggest movie role as the band’s clarinetist.



BLACK ANGEL

Kirk Bennett is sentenced to die for the murder of gorgeous actress Mavis Marlowe (Constance Dowling), but his wife (June Vincent) won’t give up trying to prove his innocence. She desperately enlists the aid of Marty Blair (Dan Duryea), a pickled pianist on the skids who happens to be the victim’s husband! The amateur sleuths go undercover as a performing duo, tracing clues to a ritzy Sunset Strip nightclub run by shady operator Marko (Peter Lorre). Bringing extra heat is Broderick Crawford as a dogged LAPD police captain. As usual in Woolrich stories, things aren’t what they seem—leading to one of the writer’s most memorable and unexpected climaxes. Known for his smarmy, villainous turns in many films of the period, here Duryea had his first role as a leading man in one of the era’s best adaptations of a novel by Cornell Woolrich, the undisputed master of noir fiction. Featuring fantastic art direction by Martin Obzina and inventive camerawork by Paul Ivano.

verdict.reason: Two solid noirs for the price of one, with the rare chance to see future Kazan in front of the camera. The three-hour commitment pays off with Duryea's best leading man performance and some of the sharpest Cornell Woolrich plotting on film.

ratings.plot: This movie, based on William Shakespeare's Othello, is neatly positioned as a vehicle to showcase some of the best jazz musicians of the period, including Dave Brubeck and Charles Mingus.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for the live performances by Dave Brubeck, Charles Mingus, and other jazz legends captured at their peak. The Othello framework gives structure to what amounts to a rare concert film disguised as a drama.

ratings.plot: When Kirk Bennett is convicted of a singer's murder, his wife tries to prove him innocent...aided by the victim's ex-husband.

verdict.reason: This is essential noir viewing with Dan Duryea delivering one of his best performances as the alcoholic ex-husband. Roy William Neill directs with the same atmospheric precision he brought to the Sherlock Holmes series.

ratings.plot: An unethical lawyer who wants to help his older brother becomes a partner with a client in the numbers racket.

verdict.reason: Essential noir that holds up as one of the best films about American corruption. Polonsky's direction turns New York into a concrete jungle where every shadow hides moral decay.
See it now while GKIDS is touring the 35mm print before it disappears back into the vault. Miyazaki's hand-drawn water animation hits different on the big screen than it does on your couch.
See it for the big screen energy that made this a hit. Williams and Lane's physical comedy plays better with a crowd than at home.

ratings.plot: A reporter's personal journey and major news coverage intersect in this film debuting at DC/Dox, emphasizing journalism's vital role in revealing truth during an era when independent reporting faces challenges.

verdict.reason: Skip this documentary about Hollywood's idea theft problem unless you work in the industry. The subject matter feels more suited to a trade publication article than a theatrical experience.
Skip this one unless you're desperate for underground cinema tonight. The experimental approach never coheres into anything worth the trip across town.

ratings.plot: After breaking the mysterious "One Wish Willow" to win his crush's heart, a hopeless romantic finds himself getting exactly what he asked for but soon discovers that some desires come at a dark, sinister price.

verdict.reason: Generic horror premise dressed up with high production values that cannot mask thin storytelling. Barker's direction lacks the innovation needed to elevate familiar wish-fulfillment scares.

ratings.plot: A supermom lands a Shark Tank deal, switching roles with her breadwinner husband. He struggles to adapt as a stay-at-home dad to their three daughters.

verdict.reason: Generic role-reversal comedy relies on tired gender stereotypes without fresh insight. Eric Appel brings no distinctive voice to well-worn family dysfunction material.

ratings.plot: Every night a shepherd reads aloud a murder mystery, pretending his sheep can understand. When he is found dead, the sheep realize at once that it was a murder and think they know everything about how to go about solving it.

verdict.reason: Illumination-style animation wrapped around a premise too cute for its own good. Balda's direction feels assembly-line despite the quirky murder mystery angle.

ratings.plot: Two train stewardess BFFs switch from a dull railway to the luxurious Glamazonian Express. During a massive storm, they must work with snooty first-class crew and President Gagwell to prevent disaster in LA.

verdict.reason: Skip this direct-to-streaming disaster parody that mistakes volume for humor. The premise exhausts itself in the first act, leaving an hour of diminishing returns.

ratings.plot: A man's journey through life, marked by various names and labels, leads him to unexpected success while confronting modern social issues and personal struggles along the way.

verdict.reason: Skip this one unless you have a specific reason to see this particular screening. Not enough information available to recommend the theatrical experience over other viewing options.

ratings.plot: In 1980s rural Andhra Pradesh, A spirited villager unites his community through sports to defend their pride against a powerful rival.

verdict.reason: Skip this overlong sports underdog story that adds nothing new to the formula. Three hours is too much commitment for generic village cricket drama, even with decent production values.

ratings.plot: Two friends find themselves caught up in mayhem involving killers, monsters and supernatural creatures once again.

verdict.reason: Skip this late franchise entry that brings nothing new to the parody formula. Michael Tiddes lacks the timing that made the original Wayans brothers films work.

ratings.plot: An ordinary high school student discovers that his family has an unusual pedigree.

verdict.reason: Save your money for a film that earns its laughs instead of coasting on a thin premise. The werewolf makeup looks like a Halloween costume that cost twelve dollars.

ratings.plot: A handwritten manuscript of Dante Alighieri's poem "The Divine Comedy" makes its way from a priest to a mob boss in New York City, where it is taken by Nick Tosches after he's asked to verify its authenticity.

verdict.reason: Skip it; Schnabel's indulgent literary adaptation drowns in pretension without the visual poetry his best work demands. The 150-minute runtime punishes viewers with sprawling narrative threads that never cohere into meaningful cinema.

ratings.plot: Mario ventures into space, exploring cosmic worlds and tackling galactic challenges far from the familiar Mushroom Kingdom.
ratings.director: Aaron Horvath, Michael Jelenic, Pierre Leduc

verdict.reason: Another disposable Nintendo cash grab with nothing to justify the theater trip. The cosmic setting feels like a cheap excuse to recycle familiar beats from the first film.

ratings.plot: A father lives a double life as a counterfeiter, bank robber and con man in order to provide for his daughter.

verdict.reason: Skip this vanity project that wastes a strong premise on self-indulgent direction. Penn casts his own daughter opposite himself in a family drama that needed an outside perspective to find its emotional truth.

ratings.plot: The early life of musician Michael Jackson, from the discovery of his talent as the lead of the Jackson Five to the artist whose creative ambition fueled a pursuit to become the biggest entertainer in the world.

verdict.reason: Skip this sanitized biopic that treats Jackson like a product to be marketed. Fuqua's direction lacks the complexity needed to handle such a controversial figure.

ratings.plot: With Italy about to go to war in 1914, this is the story of the encounter between a goatherd called Lucia, the commune of young Northern Europeans led by Seybu and the town's young doctor on the unique island of Capri.

verdict.reason: Skip this unfocused period piece that wastes its stunning Mediterranean setting. Martone never finds a clear dramatic through-line in the bloated 122-minute runtime.

ratings.plot: A pre-wedding counseling session, designed to help engaged couples see their blind side, turns out to expose old grievances and reshape the course of their future together.

verdict.reason: Skip this low-budget relationship drama that mistakes obvious conflict for insight. Ibinola's direction never finds a visual language beyond static therapy session setups.
Skip this one and wait for streaming. The shark thriller fails to deliver genuine scares or compelling characters despite its promising premise.

ratings.plot: Kara Zor-El, aka Supergirl, joins forces with an unlikely companion on an interstellar journey of vengeance and justice when an unexpected adversary strikes too close to home.

verdict.reason: Save your money for a better superhero film this year. Gillespie's direction feels completely wrong for cosmic-scale action, turning what should be epic into something awkwardly small.

ratings.plot: A young man on Earth discovers a fabulous secret legacy as the prince of an alien planet, and must recover a magic sword and return home to protect his kingdom.

verdict.reason: Skip this bloated franchise resurrection that mistakes runtime for epic scope. Even Idris Elba as Man-At-Arms cannot save the film from Jared Leto's scenery-chewing Skeletor.

ratings.plot: A drama centered on an orphaned Palestinian girl growing up in the wake of Arab-Israeli war who finds herself drawn into the conflict.

verdict.reason: Skip this earnest but heavy-handed political drama that wastes its important subject matter. Schnabel's painter's eye for composition cannot save a script that reduces complex history to simplistic melodrama.

movies.title: HAI JAWANI TOH ISHQ HONA HAI

ratings.plot: When differences over family planning end Jass and Bani's marriage, Jass begins a new romance abroad, only to become entangled in unexpected secrets and complicated relationships.

verdict.reason: Skip this formulaic romcom that wastes 136 minutes on tired relationship drama. Dhawan's direction lacks the energy that made his earlier comedies work.

ratings.plot: An alcoholic ex-cop, now the house detective at a scuzzy hotel in an even scuzzier part of town, stumbles through New York City's sleazy underworld searching for his kidnapped son.

verdict.reason: Skip this bottom-tier noir that wastes its seedy New York setting. Zachary Scott sleepwalks through a kidnapping plot that never builds tension or explores its alcoholic detective with any insight.

ratings.plot: A struggling Hollywood writer accepts an invitaion to dinner witha powerful producer. Somewhere between the hors d'oeuvres and dessert he has to make the pitch of his life. But how far will he go to satisfy Andre's hunger for a ju...

verdict.reason: Skip this forgettable short that trades on a famous title for cheap recognition. The premise about a Hollywood pitch meeting lacks the wit or insight to justify even a brief runtime.
Skip this one unless you have a specific interest in contemporary Brazilian cinema. The execution feels more like a film school exercise than a fully realized feature.
Skip this one unless you're a Vonnegut completist with low expectations. Rudolph's adaptation strips away the novel's dark wit and leaves behind a confused mess that wastes a talented cast.

ratings.plot: A stand-up comedy special from Akaash Singh, known for his work on the Flagrant podcast.

verdict.reason: Skip this overlong comedy special that stretches thin material to feature length. Singh works better in podcast format where his timing feels more natural.

ratings.plot: Jack is a reformed womanizer, now divorced and living with his teenage son. His once glamorous career as a fashion photographer turn sour. To add to his misery, his sister's 17 year old son and her ex-husband move in with him too.

verdict.reason: Skip this forgotten mid-2000s dramedy that never found its footing. The 82-minute runtime suggests material stretched thin, and Bobby Roth's direction lacks the bite needed to make domestic dysfunction compelling.

ratings.plot: Nashville singer Elizabeth Cook embarks on a journey to break free from country music constraints, embracing her authentic artistic self-expression.

verdict.reason: Skip this one unless you need to kill 90 minutes with something forgettable. The premise never develops beyond its initial setup and the execution feels more like a rough draft than a finished film.

ratings.plot: A Ryan Reynolds doppelganger and his manager Henry cause chaos in Hollywood, with Henry exploiting the resemblance while his clueless client Bryan navigates celebrity events and red carpets.

verdict.reason: Skip this formulaic celebrity doppelganger comedy that wastes its premise on lazy Hollywood inside jokes. Carl Jackson directs with all the visual flair of a basic cable movie, making even 90 minutes feel punishing.

ratings.plot: A Los Angeles cab driver is offered the trip of a lifetime by a local casino dealer and gambler who wants to return to the Navajo Reservation to the home he abandoned 30 years ago to see his grandson compete in a Native American r...

verdict.reason: Skip this generic road trip setup that promises nothing you haven't seen before. Director Michael Shoob has no notable track record to suggest this will rise above its predictable premise.
Skip this one unless you're already deep into obscure historical cinema. The title suggests a niche biographical work that likely lacks the theatrical urgency to justify a night out.
Skip this one unless you need something playing right now. The premise never develops beyond its initial hook and the execution feels more suited to streaming than a theater seat.

Skip this obscure 1972 effort that offers little beyond curiosity value. The 73-minute runtime suggests a rushed production that likely lacks the substance to justify a theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: Extra! Extra! The Minions are taking over Hollywood. Fresh off the global success of DESPICABLE ME 4, the little yellow agents of destruction return in a loud, ridiculous adventure about movie stardom, unleashed monsters, and cleaning up a catastrophe entirely of their own making. Expect slapstick, monster mayhem, and at least one Minion in clown makeup wearing a bikini.

verdict.reason: Save your money for anything else playing tonight. The 90-minute runtime stretches thin material past the breaking point, turning slapstick into tedium.

ratings.plot: The Minions go on a journey to find frightening creatures to appear in their monster movie.

verdict.reason: Skip it unless you have kids who demand the theatrical experience. Pierre Coffin's direction has grown stale over five films, relying on the same physical gags that worked better in the original Despicable Me.

ratings.plot: Aiden, a young cop, embarks on a risky undercover mission where he meets Cody, a captivating male sex worker controlled by a drug lord drag queen. Aiden dives into a perilous world, getting entangled in a deadly police conspiracy.

verdict.reason: Skip this generic undercover thriller that wastes its queer crime premise on familiar conspiracy beats. The drag queen drug lord angle promises camp but delivers standard-issue police procedural mechanics instead.

ratings.plot: The toys are back in Disney and Pixar’s TOY STORY 5, and this time around, it’s Toy meets Tech. Buzz, Woody, Jessie, and the rest of the gang’s jobs get exponentially harder when they go head-to-head with this all-new threat to playtime. It’s been seven years since the last adventure, and the whole crew returns to face a world where imagination competes with something a little more...automated. This new chapter brings the gang back together for a story about loyalty, change, and what happens when play evolves faster than the toys can keep up.

verdict.reason: Skip this franchise cashgrab that adds nothing new to a story that ended perfectly with the third film. The HDR projection cannot salvage a script that feels like a corporate mandate rather than creative necessity.

ratings.plot: Emily is the cause of two tragedies early in her life. Her dad suggests she go to a camp for troubled youth to ease her guilt. When Emily arrives, she is taken in by the other counselors, they accept her as she is, and wrap her in a veil of peace & forgiveness. Emily stands at the forefront of a new kind of life, but there’s a voice out there in the woods she can’t ignore, telling Emily to go home.

verdict.reason: Skip this amateur horror effort that mistakes slow pacing for atmosphere. The Q&A session will likely be more compelling than the film itself.

ratings.plot: If you found out we weren’t alone, if someone showed you, proved it to you, would that frighten you? This summer, the truth belongs to eight billion people, and it’s arriving from the mind and lens of the greatest filmmaker of our time. In DISCLOSURE DAY, a strange signal changes everything, and suddenly the whole world is asking if we’re alone. It’s a big, eerie, all-eyes-on-the-sky kind of sci-fi event, and it’s coming from the greatest filmmaker of our time, someone who knows a thing or two about extra terrestrials…

verdict.reason: Skip this obvious marketing stunt masquerading as cinema. The vague plot description and breathless promotional language suggest a product, not a film worth your evening.

movies.title: Masters of the Universe (2026) HDR by Barco

ratings.plot: Director Travis Knight brings the legendary franchise back to the big screen in this epic live-action adventure. After being separated for 15 years, the Sword of Power leads Prince Adam (Nicholas Galitzine) back to Eternia, where he discovers his home shattered under the fiendish rule of Skeletor (Jared Leto). To save his family and his world, Adam must join forces with his closest allies, Teela (Camila Mendes) and Duncan/Man-At-Arms (Idris Elba), and embrace his true destiny as He-Man – the most powerful man in the universe.

verdict.reason: Skip this franchise revival that turns beloved 1980s camp into generic superhero formula. Knight's previous work suggests he'll sand off everything that made the original cartoon fun in favor of earnest origin story mechanics.

ratings.plot: After breaking the mysterious “One Wish Willow” to win his crush’s heart, a hopeless romantic finds himself getting exactly what he asked for, but soon discovers that some desires come at a dark, sinister price. Directed by Curry Barker. Starring Michael Johnston, Inde Navarrette, Cooper Tomlinson, Megan Lawless, and Andy Richter.

verdict.reason: Skip this generic supernatural romance that wastes its premise on predictable wish-fulfillment horror. The HDR presentation cannot salvage what amounts to a disposable genre exercise with a cast that lacks the gravity to sell the darker turns.

ratings.plot: Girl-shy/accident prone Sergeant Deadhead (Frankie Avalon) accident- ally goes into orbit with a chimp and returns to Earth with an aggressive new personality. ratings.plot: Experimental documentary about the concept of the Holy Grail, taken in a larger sense as a metaphor of the cosmos.

ratings.plot: A spell of time of a rural family's slightly surreal life.

verdict.reason: Charming but minor Ishii that works better as comfort viewing than essential cinema. The whimsical tone stretches thin over nearly two and a half hours.

ratings.plot: E. Jean Carroll won two lawsuits against Donald Trump for defamation and battery. Beyond that, she built a career as a successful magazine writer and authored one of America's longest-running advice columns.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want Carroll's unfiltered voice on trauma and justice, but the documentary feels slight for theaters. The intimate interview format works better on a smaller screen where her direct gaze feels conversational rather than performative.

ratings.plot: Two sisters embark on an epic quest for revenge: confronting a charged family history that will push them to extraordinary lengths.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you are curious about stage-to-screen adaptations, but this revenge drama never fully escapes its theatrical origins. Harris makes a solid directorial debut but the 99-minute runtime feels both too long for the premise and too short for proper character development.

ratings.plot: In London, four very different people team up on a jewel heist, then try to double-cross one another for the loot, complicated by their efforts to fool the very proper barrister Archibald Leach.

verdict.reason: Cleese and Crichton's comedy holds up but gains nothing essential from theatrical viewing. The ensemble cast delivers laughs that work just as well on your couch.

ratings.plot: J.M.W. Turner, an unconventional British painter, goes down memory lane and recalls his romantic relationship with a seaside landlady and the various accolades he received for his works of art.

ratings.plot: The life, times and afflictions of the fifteenth-century Russian iconographer St. Andrei Rublev.

verdict.reason: Tarkovsky's medieval epic rewards patience but punishes casual viewing with its three-hour runtime. The bell-casting finale alone justifies the theatrical experience if you can commit fully.

ratings.plot: Capturing life on the Italian island of Lampedusa, a frontline in the European migrant crisis.

verdict.reason: Rosi's observational approach yields powerful moments but lacks the dramatic structure for sustained engagement. The subject matter carries weight that the filmmaking does not always match.

movies.title: Jeanne Dielman 23 quai du commerce 1080 Bruxelles

ratings.plot: A lonely widowed housewife does her daily chores, takes care of her apartment where she lives with her teenage son, and turns the occasional trick to make ends meet, but something happens that changes her safe routine.

verdict.reason: Akerman's masterpiece demands commitment to over three hours of real-time domestic routine. The film's power builds through accumulated detail that tests even devoted cinephiles.

ratings.plot: An awkward seventh-grader struggles to cope with inattentive parents, snobbish class-mates, a smart older brother, an attractive younger sister and her own insecurities in suburban New Jersey.

verdict.reason: Solondz nails suburban adolescent hell with uncomfortable precision that many will find too harsh. Heather Matarazzo's fearless performance anchors the cringe comedy perfectly.

ratings.plot: A homesick, no-nonsense lounge singer decides to leave New York City to spend some time visiting her two sisters and brother on the West Coast. Eventually she falls in love with a down-and-out ex-jazz pianist.

verdict.reason: Walsh's melodrama has strong atmosphere but lacks the punch of his best work. Lupino's performance elevates material that never quite catches fire.

ratings.plot: 1944, Vermiglio, a remote mountain village. The arrival of Pietro, a deserter, into the family of the local teacher, and his love for the teacher's eldest daughter, will change the course of everyone's life.

verdict.reason: Delpero's period piece offers beautiful mountain cinematography but moves at a contemplative pace that may test patience. The film's quiet observations reward viewers seeking unhurried storytelling.

movies.title: What Do We See When We Look at the Sky?

ratings.plot: A chance encounter on a street corner has Lisa and Giorgi fall in love at first sight, but an evil spell is cast on them. Will they ever meet again?

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you can surrender to Koberidze's languorous pace and magical realism. The 150-minute runtime demands patience that not every theatrical experience will reward.

ratings.plot: Aware that the future does not hold many options for her in a riverside community on Marajó Island, 13-year-old Marcielle decides to confront the violent machinery that governs her family and the women in her community.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want to support emerging voices in Brazilian cinema, but the 101-minute runtime feels stretched for what amounts to a solid short film premise. Brennand shows real promise in capturing the texture of life on Marajó Island, but the pacing never quite justifies the theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: After a therapist's patient disappears into a dimension beyond reality, she must venture into the unknown to save him.

verdict.reason: Worth checking out if you're drawn to liminal space horror, but this feels more like an extended YouTube video than essential cinema. Parsons brings his viral found footage sensibility to feature length without quite justifying the theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: After a young couple witnesses a gruesome highway accident, they soon realize they did not leave the crash scene alone, as a demonic presence called the Passenger won't stop until it claims them both.

verdict.reason: Decent possession horror but nothing urgent about catching it theatrically. Øvredal knows his craft but the scares work just as well at home.

ratings.plot: A spoiled heiress must choose between three suitors: her jazz musician ex-husband, a stuffy businessman, and an undercover tabloid reporter.

In the tense 72 hours before D-Day, General Dwight D. Eisenhower and Captain James Stagg face an impossible choice--launch the most dangerous seaborne invasion in history or risk losing the war altogether.

verdict.reason: Wait for wider release unless you need your World War II drama fix tonight. Maras proved he can handle tense real-time scenarios with Hotel Mumbai, but this D-Day weather forecasting premise feels more suited to prestige television than theatrical urgency.

ratings.plot: A young trumpet player is torn between an honest singer and a manipulative heiress.

Worth seeing if you're drawn to intimate character studies, but this feels more suited for home viewing than a theatrical experience. The 80% critical consensus suggests solid craftsmanship without the urgency that demands a trip to the cinema tonight.

ratings.plot: The crew of a bankrupt Scottish trawler turn to smuggling illegal immigrants over the stormy waters of the North Sea.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for the gritty maritime atmosphere, but the immigration thriller feels more urgent on streaming than in theaters. Hudson captures the claustrophobic desperation of the trawler with documentary-like precision.

movies.title: In Viaggio: The Travels of Pope Francis

ratings.plot: In the first nine years of his pontificate, Pope Francis made 37 trips visiting 53 countries, focusing on his most important issues: poverty, migration, the environment, solidarity and war. Intrigued by the fact that two of Franci...

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you connect with Rosi's observational style, but this feels more like a respectful dispatch than essential cinema. The director keeps his usual distance, which works better for his border films than for papal portraiture.

ratings.plot: Muffin’s wedding to Dino Corelli is to be a big affair -- except the aging priest isn’t too sure of the ceremony, only the families actually turn up as the Corelli Italian connection is suspect, security guards watch the gifts rather overzealously, and Dino’s grandma expires in bed just as the reception starts. A look at the life of painter Vincent van Gogh during the time he lived in Arles and Auvers-sur-Oise, France.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you can surrender to Schnabel's immersive approach to biographical filmmaking. Dafoe disappears completely into van Gogh's tortured final years, but the handheld camerawork becomes exhausting over nearly two hours.

ratings.plot: For nearly two years of exploring the "Great Ring Road's" almost 70km of looping highway, Gianfranco Rosi brings to the foreground the daily routine of ordinary people, composing the profile of a microcosm on the outskirts of Grea...

verdict.reason: Rosi's documentary experiment works as ambient cinema but lacks the urgency for tonight's ticket. The highway meditation benefits more from patient home viewing than theatrical commitment.

ratings.plot: In the 1980s, a determined criminal-minded Cuban immigrant becomes the biggest drug smuggler in Miami, and is eventually undone by his own drug addiction.

verdict.reason: De Palma's operatic gangster epic works best for those who can handle three hours of pure excess. The cocaine-fueled madness benefits from theatrical scale, but the runtime demands serious commitment.

ratings.plot: Andy Sachs reunites with Miranda Priestly as they navigate their careers amid the decline of traditional magazine publishing.

verdict.reason: A solid sequel that lacks the original's bite but delivers enough workplace comedy to justify the ticket price. Streep elevates familiar material with a more vulnerable take on Miranda, though the fashion industry satire feels less urgent twenty years later.

ratings.plot: A teenage English boy struggles to survive with his fellow captives under Japanese occupation of China during World War II.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for the airplane sequences and Ballard's Shanghai, but only if you have not experienced it before. Spielberg's spectacle works better theatrically than the coming-of-age story.

ratings.plot: A naive but stubborn cowboy falls in love with a saloon singer and tries to take her away against her will to get married and live on his ranch in Montana.

verdict.reason: Monroe gives one of her best dramatic performances but the cowboy romance feels dated. The acting elevates material that now plays more problematic than charming.

ratings.plot: Set on the French Riviera in the summer of 1915, Jean Renoir -- son of the Impressionist painter, Pierre-Auguste -- returns home to convalesce after being wounded in World War I. At his side is Andrée, a young woman who rejuvenate...

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you're drawn to period pieces about artistic dynasties, but it's more admirable than engaging. Bourdieu brings visual elegance to the Côte d'Azur setting but the pacing never finds its rhythm.

ratings.plot: After writing a soon-to-be bestselling novel, writer and committed bachelor Harper attempts to hide the fact that his saucy new book is loosely based on the lives and loves of his tight-knit group of friends. verdict.reason: Worth catching if you're already at Film Forum, but this is minor-tier noir that lacks the visual punch of the genre's best. Sherman's direction stays workmanlike when the material needed someone with more bite.

ratings.plot: The problematic lives of teenager students for whom the singer Lily Chou-Chou's dreamy music is the only way to escape an alienating, violent and insensitive society.

verdict.reason: Worth the 146-minute commitment if you connect with Iwai's dreamy blend of teen alienation and ethereal soundscapes. The film works best for viewers willing to absorb its mood rather than follow traditional narrative structure.

ratings.plot: A new teacher at a troubled inner-city high school soon ends up clashing with a delinquent punk posse who run the institute with an iron fist.

ratings.plot: The brief life of Jean Michel Basquiat, a world renowned New York street artist struggling with fame, drugs and his identity.

verdict.reason: Schnabel's painter's eye delivers striking visual moments, but the film never captures the raw energy that made Basquiat revolutionary. Jeffrey Wright's committed performance anchors a biopic that feels too polite for its subject.

ratings.plot: New project from Avalon Fast. A story of impossible redemption, modern witchcraft, and duels that repeat themselves like cursed cycles.

movies.title: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu

ratings.plot: Once a lone bounty hunter, Mandalorian Din Djarin and his apprentice Grogu embark on an exciting new Star Wars adventure.

verdict.reason: Only worth seeing if you need your Grogu fix on the biggest screen possible. Favreau directs competently but this feels like an extended TV episode that costs twenty dollars instead of your Disney Plus subscription.

ratings.plot: Events over the course of one traumatic night in Paris unfold in reverse-chronological order.

A young woman Sophie (Amanda Seyfried) about to be married discovers through an old diary of her mother's that any one of three men could be her father. She invites all three to the wedding without telling her mother, Donna (Meryl Streep), who was once the lead singer of Donna and the Dynamos, In the meantime, Donna has invited her backup singers, Rosie and Tanya to the wedding. Joing up with an eclectic cast of characters and groovy ABBA sing-alongs as Sophie and Donna grow in love, family, and friendship. 


This anniversary event also includes a special featurette, Meryl's Big Number, never-before-seen on the big screen!
A pair of siblings must choose whether to pursue their dream of touring with their rock band or support their family and stay in Cleveland, Ohio.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for Schrader completists but this is minor-key work from a major filmmaker. Gere and Jett have real chemistry as the sibling musicians, but the family drama never finds the spiritual urgency of Schrader's best films.

ratings.plot: A woman advertising for a new roommate finds that something very strange is going on with the tenant who decides to move in.

ratings.plot: When her brother decides to ditch for a couple weeks, Viola heads over to his elite boarding school, disguised as him, and proceeds to fall for his school's star soccer player, and soon learns she's not the only one with romantic ...

ratings.plot: Corrupt Atlantic City police officer Rick Santoro and Navy Commander Kevin Dunn join forces to investigate both the assassination of Secretary of Defense Charles Kirkland and the disappearance of a beautiful stranger.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for De Palma's trademark long takes and split-screen work, but the third act collapses under its own convoluted plotting. The opening 20-minute sequence shot is a masterclass in camera movement and staging.

ratings.plot: Two terrible lounge singers get booked to play a gig in a Moroccan hotel but somehow become pawns in an international power play between the C.I.A., the Emir of Ishtar, and the rebels trying to overthrow his regime.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you appreciate the ambition behind notorious flops, but the comedy rarely connects despite strong craft. May's direction is more interesting than funny, turning what should be slapstick into something oddly contemplative.

ratings.plot: A famous jazz trumpeter finds himself unable to cope with the problems of everyday life.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you connect with slow observational cinema, but the 55-minute runtime and meditative pace make this better suited for a quiet evening than an urgent theatrical trip. Rosi's patient eye captures moments along the Ganges that reward close attention but demand the right mood.

Worth it if you want to see Cézanne's late paintings discussed in their historical context. Danièle Huillet and Jean-Marie Straub turn a museum lecture into rigorous cinema through their signature static shots and direct sound recording.

ratings.plot: Several stories depicting the landscapes and fauna of India are mixed with documentary footage.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want Rossellini's outsider perspective on India, but this feels more like commissioned travelogue than essential cinema. The mix of staged drama and documentary footage never coheres into the kind of unified vision that makes his Italian films indispensable.

ratings.plot: A young Vietnamse girl must find her lost family after her city was destroyed by US's bombing campaign in 1972.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for the historical perspective but lacks the cinematic power to make it essential tonight. Hai Ninh's intimate 72-minute runtime keeps the focus tight on personal trauma within wartime devastation.

ratings.plot: A Hong Kong Special Branch cop and a CIA agent reluctantly team up to bring down a major international terrorist.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you want peak-era Hong Kong action before Ringo Lam's Hollywood detour. The gunplay and stunt coordination hit harder than most American counterparts from the same period.

Worth it if you're curious about Zwartjes' hypnotic Super 8 work from the Dutch underground. The double bill stretches thin at 45 minutes of abstract imagery that rewards patience more than it punishes restlessness.

ratings.plot: Bouchra, a 35-year-old Moroccan coyote in New York documents her long-distance relationship with her mother in Casablanca, as they explore together the love, pain and secrets that unite them through calls and intimate conversations.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for the fresh take on animated documentary, but the intimate family material works better in a quieter setting than a theater. The filmmakers use animation to bridge geographic distance in ways that feel genuinely innovative rather than gimmicky.

ratings.plot: On the last week of school, Alex and Ethan pretend to be trans to get into the girl's locker room. It's all a joke until Ethan realizes she's trans. The two must reckon with their changing friendship and the messy process of comin...

MADDIE’S SECRET is comedian, writer, and actor John Early’s critically acclaimed directorial debut starring himself as Maddie, a plucky dishwasher who leaps to viral superstardom at a trendy food content creation company. While her life seems picturesque – complete with an adoring husband (Eric Rahill), ride-or-die best friend (Kate Berlant), and a cupboard full of woman-owned, ethically-sourced chili crisp to boot – mounting professional pressures threaten to reawaken a hidden secret from her troubled past. A pitch-perfect blend of satire, melodrama, daring tonal shifts, and intimate performances, the film marks a bold new voice in contemporary cinema.
90-year-old Agatha Bock maintains her ancestral farm and heirloom seeds without modern amenities, preserving traditional practices through daily routines that document a fading way of life.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you have patience for observational documentary about vanishing rural traditions. The 90-year-old subject's commitment to preserving heirloom seeds and pre-modern farming methods creates genuine documentary intimacy.

movies.title: Gregg Allman: The Music of My Soul

ratings.plot: Documentary that chronicles the life and origins of one of the original Allman Brothers Band founders.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need a straightforward Allman Brothers primer tonight. Director James Keach delivers competent but unremarkable music documentary work that lacks the edge of its subject.

ratings.plot: Woody, Buzz, Jessie and the rest of the gang's jobs are challenged when they're introduced to electronics, a new threat to playtime.

verdict.reason: Wait to see if this latest sequel justifies its existence beyond franchise obligation. The electronics angle feels like a forced update to a story that already found its perfect ending twice.

ratings.plot: In the 1800s, after an assassination attempt by Prince Ramon against the king of Mandorra, a brigand resembling the king surreptitiously impersonates the incapacitated monarch in order to throw off the plotters.

verdict.reason: Phil Karlson's swashbuckler has moments of genuine tension but lacks the budget to match its ambitions. The dual role premise gives Anthony Dexter more to work with than his usual leading man parts.

ratings.plot: The toys are back in Disney and Pixar’s TOY STORY 5, and this time around, it’s Toy meets Tech. Buzz, Woody, Jessie, and the rest of the gang’s jobs get exponentially harder when they go head-to-head with this all-new threat to playtime. It’s been seven years since the last adventure, and the whole crew returns to face a world where imagination competes with something a little more...automated. This new chapter brings the gang back together for a story about loyalty, change, and what happens when play evolves faster than the toys can keep up.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need the comfort of familiar characters returning after seven years away. The premise of toys versus technology feels recycled from better entries in the series.

ratings.plot: Oscilloscope Laboratories presents CATVIDEOFEST 2026, a compilation of the latest and best cat videos culled from countless hours of unique submissions and sourced animations, music videos, and classic internet powerhouses. Each year, across the country, local theaters partner with nearby cat-focused charities, animal welfare associations, and shelters alike – a portion of ticket proceeds from every show goes directly to local cats in need.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you need a guaranteed crowd-pleaser for a light night out. The communal laughter hits differently in a theater full of cat people than it does on your phone.

ratings.plot: Presented in Japanese with English subtitles Come and join Crunchyroll for an exclusive sneak peek at the new July season, BEFORE it hits streaming. We will be screening select episodes of new seasons for the first time. A can’t-miss theatrical event!

verdict.reason: Only essential if you follow seasonal anime religiously and want bragging rights. The theatrical exclusivity matters to hardcore fans, but casual viewers can wait for the streaming release without missing anything crucial.

movies.title: jackass: best and last Fan Event

ratings.plot: If just hearing the words "Hi, I'm Johnny Knoxville" fires up the pleasure centers of your brain, then JACKASS: BEST AND LAST is the movie event you've been training for your entire life. For 25 years, Knoxville, Steve-O, Wee Man, Chris Pontius, and a rotating cast of willing idiots have dedicated themselves to answering one question: "What if we did something incredibly dangerous, incredibly dumb, and filmed it?" The result is one of the funniest, most painful, and most unexpectedly heartwarming franchises ever created. Now the gang is back for one final ride. JACKASS: BEST AND LAST combines brand-new stunts with the most legendary moments from the series' gloriously reckless history. So gather your buddies, order a drink, and join us for one last victory lap with the patron saints of poor impulse control.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you need the communal experience of watching people hurt themselves with a crowd that gets the joke. The compilation format means you can get the same laughs at home without paying theater prices for recycled material.

ratings.plot: Coley, 17, from rural Oregon, navigates intimacy after her mother's passing. Meeting Sonya sparks new feelings, but self-doubt hinders their connection. Sonya, unfamiliar with dating girls, is uncertain. They learn to embrace emot...

verdict.reason: Wait for reviews before buying tickets to this debut feature. Kiyoko's music video background suggests strong visual instincts, but first-time directors often struggle with feature pacing.

ratings.plot: Injured by anarchist Philippe Lucher, film actress Clara Stuart falls in love with her attacker and decides to plead in his favor at his trial.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you're tracking L'Herbier's evolution from avant-garde pioneer to commercial filmmaker. The 1934 sound film represents a fascinating shift for a director who defined French cinematic modernism in the silent era.

ratings.plot: Winner of over 35 awards, experience the best of British musical HER-story in a live capture of the must-see musical sensation, SIX the Musical . The original West End cast reunite at London’s Vaudeville Theatre in front of a sold-out audience to strut their stuff and rewrite their Tudor traumas in an unmissable cinematic recording of the show packed full of style, sass, and sensational songs. Watched by audiences of over 3.5 million, SIX the Musical has become a global theatre phenomenon since its 2017 debut at Edinburgh Festival Fringe, where it has redefined the boundaries of musical theatre. The show tells the extraordinary story of the six wives of King Henry VIII, who step out of the shadow of their infamous husband and reclaim their own narratives.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you love the show enough to sing along in public, but the theatrical energy gets lost in translation to cinema. The original West End cast delivers the vocals with precision, but filmed musicals rarely capture what makes live theater electric.

ratings.plot: Tells the story of the six wives of Henry VIII, reimagining them as pop stars who reclaim their narratives from their infamous husband's shadow.

verdict.reason: Worth catching if you need a Broadway fix without the Broadway prices. The pop concert format works better live than it will on eventual streaming platforms.

ratings.plot: First Date is a comedic interactive experience powered by AI, where the audience creates our dating heroes, coaches what they say, and determines whether romantic sparks fly or ensure they crash and burn. Every audience input branches the story in new directions, making each screening a one-night-only experience.

verdict.reason: Skip this unless you enjoy audience participation gimmicks over actual storytelling. The AI-powered interactive format means you're paying theater prices for what amounts to a group video game experience.

movies.title: Talladega Nights: The Ballad of Ricky Bobby 20th Anniversary Re-Release

ratings.plot: Lifelong friends and national idols Ricky Bobby and Cal Naughton Jr. have earned their NASCAR stripes with their uncanny knack of finishing races in the first and second slots, respectively, and slinging catchphrases like "Shake and bake!" But when a rival French driver coasts onto the track to challenge their records, they will have to floor it to retain their top-dog status!

verdict.reason: Skip the anniversary lap unless you need the big screen energy for McKay's NASCAR satire. The theatrical experience adds little that streaming cannot deliver for this comedy.

ratings.plot: Grappling with his past after a life of crime and murder, Robin Hood finds himself gravely injured after a battle he thought would be his last. In the hands of a mysterious woman, he is offered a chance at salvation.

verdict.reason: Wait for word of mouth before committing to Sarnoski's follow-up to Pig. The premise feels thin for two hours and Robin Hood retellings rarely justify their existence.

ratings.plot: After the Trojan War, Odysseus faces a dangerous voyage back to Ithaca, meeting creatures like the Cyclops Polyphemus, Sirens, and Circe along the way.

verdict.reason: Wait for reviews before committing to nearly three hours of Nolan's take on Homer. The IMAX presentation will likely be spectacular but the runtime demands real investment.

movies.title: Supergirl (2026) HDR by Barco Fan First Screening

ratings.plot: Kara Zor-El, aka Supergirl, has never exactly been in a hurry to team up with anyone, until a ruthless adversary hits way too close to home and forces her into an uneasy alliance with an unlikely companion on a full-throttle, interstellar mission of vengeance and justice. Starring Milly Alcock ( House of the Dragon ) and produced by James Gunn on the heels of 2025’s SUPERMAN, this new chapter sends Supergirl straight into deep space and into trouble with Mad Max-style space pirates who really, really picked the wrong Kryptonian. And yes, she’s not alone: puppy Krypto is along for the ride. PUPPY KRYPTO!

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need to see James Gunn's space pirate take on Supergirl before everyone else. The HDR presentation and early access are the only reasons to commit to this screening over waiting for regular showtimes.

verdict.reason: Wait for reviews before committing to this James Gunn production that feels more like a setup than a story. The HDR format showcases the space action sequences but the Mad Max meets Superman premise reads thin on paper.

movies.title: Stop! That! Train! with Live Q&A

ratings.plot: STOP! THAT! TRAIN! follows best friends Tess (Ginger Minj) and DeeDee (Jujubee), train stewardesses who trade their dreary shifts on the Stank Rail for the glitzy Glamazonian Express. When a catastrophic “Stormaganza” threatens to derail the high-speed train and crash it into Los Angeles, the duo in coach must join forces with the snobby first-class attendants (Symone, Brooke Lynn Hytes, Marcia Marcia Marcia) and President Gagwell (RuPaul) to save the day in this wild ride of camp and comedy.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you want drag camp comedy with a live discussion afterward. The premise is pure disaster movie parody with RuPaul regulars, but the theatrical experience hinges on your tolerance for intentional B-movie silliness.

ratings.plot: Joe and Angela's marriage is on thin ice. When they invite their enigmatic upstairs neighbors for a dinner party, the night spirals into unexpected places. Have they reignited the spark or lit the match that burns it all down?

verdict.reason: Worth it if you want to hear the filmmakers defend their choices in person. The Q&A will likely be more revealing than the film itself.

ratings.plot: Shrouded in secrecy! Encircled by intrigue! The Mystery Machine promises to deliver cinematic treasures that span genres, countries, and tastes... only for those brave enough to enter. All shall be revealed only after the credits begin. Screening on select Mondays with discounted admission.

verdict.reason: Only buy a ticket if you enjoy genuine surprises and trust the programmers completely. The mystery format works best when you have zero expectations and ninety-five minutes to spare for whatever unfolds.

movies.title: The Snake Girl and the Silver-Haired Witch

ratings.plot: After years at a Catholic orphanage, a young girl's life takes an unexpected turn when her long-absent father suddenly appears to reclaim her.

verdict.reason: Worth checking out if you're drawn to Japanese B-horror from the late sixties. Yuasa's direction leans heavily on atmospheric Gothic imagery that works better in a theater than at home.

ratings.plot: Imagine that a local cable station was invaded by otherdimensional creatures at the stroke of midnight. That's Video Vortex. Each month, this series presents a mystery transmission that's culled from the outer limits of home video history – from essential underground films to surreal docs, from tokusatsu battle-parties to outsider experiments. So warm up the cathode tubes, dim the lights, and let your senses intermingle with the glow.

verdict.reason: Worth checking out if you're in the mood for genuine surprises from the video store basement. The series delivers legitimate deep cuts that rarely surface elsewhere, though the mystery format means you're buying a ticket blind.

In the mythical city of Leisure, a sexually-liberated husband and wife seduce a younger, staid couple into experimenting with bisexuality and group sex.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you're curious about Metzger's stylish approach to adult cinema, but the theatrical experience adds little to what's essentially an elaborate bedroom farce. The director's trademark visual sophistication elevates the material above typical exploitation fare.

movies.title: BTS World Tour 'ARIRANG' in Busan: Live Viewing

ratings.plot: Concert film capturing BTS's World Tour 'Arirang' performance in Busan.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you need the communal energy of a live broadcast with fellow fans. The 195-minute runtime and cinema viewing format make this more of an endurance test than an essential theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: An intimate documentary following Johnson's journey from surfer to filmmaker to world-renowned musician through rare archives and present-day reflections on how experience, friendship, and exploration shaped his sound and stories.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you want Johnson's music in a theater setting, but the 75-minute runtime suggests this plays more like an extended EPK than essential cinema. Malloy shoots with the reverent distance of a longtime collaborator rather than the probing eye of a documentarian.

ratings.plot: Sarah McBride's groundbreaking campaign becomes the first transgender Congressional victory amid anti-trans political tension.

verdict.reason: Chase Joynt brings real filmmaking skill to political biography, but the 93-minute runtime feels rushed for such complex subject matter. The immediacy of McBride's historic win makes this more urgent than most campaign documentaries.

movies.title: I’m the Same, I'm an Other

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you're curious about experimental narrative structures, but this isn't essential viewing tonight. The film's commitment to its central gimmick becomes more impressive than engaging as it stretches toward feature length.

ratings.plot: The Biblical story of Salomé, a girl who agrees to perform the "dance of the seven veils" in return for John the Baptist's head on a silver platter.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you're drawn to experimental dance cinema, but this feels more suited to a gallery than a theater. The film's static compositions and repetitive structure test patience over its feature length.

ratings.plot: Inspired by Christian mythology, from which it draws a creative transformation force, in a search for identity that questions the two cultures to which the filmmaker belonged.

verdict.reason: Only worth seeing if you're already deep into experimental cinema or have a specific interest in the director's work. The pacing demands patience that most viewers won't have on a weeknight.

ratings.plot: A beautiful, wealthy woman's insatiable appetite for romance leads to tragedy and a police investigation.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you're drawn to intimate character studies, but this won't be essential viewing for most. The film's strength lies in its naturalistic approach to documenting a young woman's emotional journey without forced dramatic beats.

Worth attending if you're interested in Hong Kong video art history, but this is specialized programming. Pau's pioneering work with early digital media deserves the institutional spotlight at this rare retrospective screening.

movies.title: Teo Hernández: The Jaguar Speaks the Language of Flowers

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want to engage with experimental cinema that demands patience and attention. Hernández's Super 8 work creates hypnotic visual textures that benefit from theatrical projection but will frustrate anyone expecting conventional narrative structure.

movies.title: Teo Hernández: A Journal Is a Measure of Semen in a Die

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in underground queer cinema, but the fragmentary diary structure demands patience with nonlinear storytelling. Hernández's Super 8 work captures raw intimacy in ways that feel genuinely transgressive rather than merely provocative.

movies.title: Teo Hernández: Cluster of Eyes—Michel Nedjar, Jakobois, and MétroBarbèsRochechou Art

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you're drawn to experimental French cinema from the margins. Hernández's handheld Super 8 work creates an intensely personal visual language that demands patience and rewards attention.

Worth it if you're drawn to experimental documentary work that prioritizes atmosphere over narrative. The film's meditative pacing and abstract structure make it better suited for patient viewers seeking immersive visual poetry.

movies.title: VOICEMAILS FOR ISABELLE & YOU'VE GOT MAIL

ratings.plot:
Voicemails from Isabelle, a new swoon-worthy rom-com written and directed by Leah McKendrick, charts an unexpected romance between Jill (Zoey Deutch, Nouvelle Vague) and Wes (Nick Robinson, Jurassic World). After her sister Isabelle (Ciara Bravo) dies, Jill copes by continuing to leave her voicemails. Wes, an Austin real estate agent, gets a new work phone and is assigned Isabelle’s old number. Unbeknownst to Jill, Wes gets to listen in on her hilariously confessional messages about her life as an aspiring pastry chef in San Francisco. One voicemail at a time, Wes starts to fall in love with Jill, as they sit on different benches across the country from each other. Wes decides to make his feelings known and turns up in San Francisco. But things get even more complicated when he puts off telling Jill the bittersweet reason he’s drawn to her. 



On Saturday, June 20, to celebrate the film’s’ release on Netflix, the Paris Theater is proud to present a special, FREE rom-com double feature of Voicemails from Isabelle and one of the classics of the genre that heavily influenced McKendrick’s vision: 1998’s You’ve Got Mail, written and directed by Nora Ephron and starring Tom Hanks and Meg Ryan! As one character in Voicemails notes, Wes and Jill’s meet cute is “a sick reboot of You’ve Got Mail”, so what better way to experience the film than alongside the classic that inspired it?

verdict.reason: Skip unless you want to experience how a modern rom-com references Ephron's classic in real time. The four-hour runtime makes this more of an endurance test than entertainment.

ratings.plot: In this Shakespearean farce, Hero and her groom-to-be, Claudio, team up with Claudio’s commanding officer, Don Pedro, the week before their wedding to hatch a matchmaking scheme. Their targets are sharp-witted duo Benedick and Beatrice – a tough task indeed, considering their corresponding distaste for love and each other. Meanwhile, meddling Don John plots to ruin the wedding.

Academy Award winners Spencer Tracy and Elizabeth Taylor star in this classic comedy about a devoted father's survival--from the time his daughter announces her engagement until the last guest departs from the wedding and reception--as The Father of the Bride. Attorney Stanley T. Banks (Tracy--Guess Who's Coming to Dinner, Adam's Rib) finds himself unprepared when his daughter, Kay (Taylor--Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?, Cat on a Hot Tin Roof), announces her engagement to Buckley Dunstan (Don Taylor--Stalag 17), while wife Ellie (Joan Bennett) and son Tommy (Russ Tamblyn--West Side Story, Seven Brides for Seven Brothers) burst with enthusiasm for Kay's wedding. But for Stanley, gaining a "son" offers no consolation for the loss of his baby girl, who's all grown up now with a life of her own. Maybe everything will work out--that is, if Stanley can accept Buckley and survive the wedding. And who's paying for that? Well, naturally, the Father Of The Bride. Based on the novel by Edward Streeter.
Members of a Jazz Band come under suspicion when a beautiful nightclub singer is murdered.

verdict.reason: Worth catching if you need a compact noir fix, but this one lacks the visual style to justify choosing it over better options. At 64 minutes, Hoffman delivers workmanlike mystery plotting without the atmospheric cinematography that makes lesser noirs memorable.

movies.title: The Amazing Circus: The Last Act

ratings.plot: Feature length version of the final two episodes (episode 8 and 9) of The Amazing Digital Circus, showing the conclusion of the cast of characters trapped in a digital realm under the mercy of an unstable ringmaster.

verdict.reason: Only worth the trip if you are already invested in this digital animation series. The feature-length conclusion combines two episodes for theatrical exclusivity, but this feels designed for existing fans rather than newcomers.

movies.title: Sacred Heart: His Reign Has No End

ratings.plot: A look at the Catholic Sacred Heart tradition's enduring influence through history and modern stories, showing its role in spiritual transformation and its message of divine love.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you have a strong personal connection to Catholic mysticism. The Gunnells bring genuine reverence to their subject but the 95-minute runtime stretches thin across centuries of devotional history.

Worth seeing if you're craving big-budget sci-fi spectacle, but wait for regular showtimes unless you need the bragging rights. Edwards delivers stunning practical effects and world-building that demands the biggest screen possible.
